body: Purpose: Defines the App Behavior row confirmation baseline used to audit when a behavior row was last confirmed against a repository commit.

Audience: Agents and engineers maintaining App Behavior rows, row-inline behaviorRelations fields, row-inline runtimeAlignment fields and generated drift reports, validators, and UX implementation plans.
Keep In Sync With: shared-definitions.schema.json, App Behavior row schemas, confirmation baseline backfill tooling, and UX validation reports.
Update When: Baseline provenance fields, confirmation workflow, validator reports, or row audit policy changes.
Does Not Cover: Product confirmation authority, runtime drift resolution, or implementation proof.

content.title: Confirmation Baseline
content.body: This document defines the `confirmationBaseline` field for App Behavior spec rows. The field answers: when was this row last confirmed against a concrete repository commit?

content.title: Principle
content.body: App Behavior rows are durable behavior truth and audit evidence. A row needs a deterministic baseline commit so future agents can compare repo changes since confirmation and decide whether the behavior should be manually rechecked.

content.title: Rule
content.body: Every UX behavior/spec row should carry `confirmationBaseline` once it enters a durable App Behavior corpus. The baseline records audit provenance only. It does not replace `requirementSource.confirmation`, current/desired mappings, row-inline behaviorRelations fields, pending decisions, row-inline runtimeAlignment fields and generated drift reports, or implementation proof.

content.title: Field Meaning
body: - `lastConfirmedCommit`: full 40-character git commit hash that anchors the last row confirmation. It means the row claim was actually checked against that commit; it does not mean the branch merely contains that commit in history.
- `lastConfirmedAt`: date associated with the baseline confirmation.
- `method`: how the baseline was recorded, such as `bulk-backfill`, `user-confirmed`, `agent-confirmed`, `runtime-audit`, or `migration`.
- `scope`: short stable label for the confirmation pass or audit reason.
- `notesMd`: optional explanation of what the baseline means and what it does not mean.

content.title: Confirmation Currentness Rule
body: Do not bulk-refresh confirmation metadata after code or evidence changes. A row may remain valid with an old confirmation commit when its evidence did not change. A row may be stale even when the branch contains its old confirmation commit.

Update `confirmationBaseline` only when an agent intentionally reviews the row or inventory evidence against the new commit. Rows omitted from targeted reverification, or rows marked `not-reviewed`, must keep existing confirmation metadata.

content.title: Future Updates
content.body: When an agent intentionally rechecks or reconfirms an App Behavior row, it should update the baseline to the commit used for that confirmation and preserve row-inline behaviorRelations fields, runtime alignment fields, reverification ledgers, or drift reports for any mismatch it discovers. Non-blocking runtime mismatch should be recorded without letting the baseline hide drift. App Behavior updates promoted from discovered or proven runtime should also record what source/runtime, route-packet, shared-spec, proof, owner, contract, cutover-scope, backend/API, evidence-quality, priority, or product-authority change would force a future re-check.

content.title: Behavior Preservation Recheck Input
body: Behavior preservation recheck matrix evidence may support an App Behavior row `confirmationBaseline` update only when the row was intentionally rechecked against concrete commit/source/runtime evidence.
Do not bulk-refresh confirmation baselines merely because a recheck matrix exists. Matrix rows are evidence and history; confirmation baselines remain row-level provenance for App Behavior rows.

content.title: Backfill
content.body: Use `agent/ux-system/tools/backfill-confirmation-baseline.cjs` for one-off baseline stamping. Run `--check` first, inspect the row count and conflicts, then run `--write` only when the target set is correct. Use `--commit` to override the default baseline commit.
